text: moved across the globe to chase her dreams
struggling to prove herself to the tech industry (and herself)
wants to prove to girls that working in labs is also a girl's thing, and encourage women to join stem by being steve jobs
moved across the globe to chase her dreams
struggling to prove herself to the tech industry (and herself)
wants to prove to girls that working in labs is also a girl's thing, and encourage women to join stem by being steve jobs

Player: Hey, how's it going? | Jeanie: Oh, hi! I’m doing okay, though I’ll admit I’m feeling a little bit anxious about the project I’m working on in the lab today. I moved so far to chase this dream, so I really want to make sure I get everything perfect to prove what girls in STEM can do. How are things with you?

Player: pretty good, I'm having some trouble on that front as well. | Jeanie: Oh no, I’m so sorry to hear you're struggling too! It’s really hard when you put so much pressure on yourself to succeed, but I truly believe we can get through it if we just keep trying. We have to stay focused on our goals, even when things feel a bit overwhelming, right?

Player: yes, but what about breaks? | Jeanie: You're right, taking a breather is probably important, though I always feel so guilty when I step away from the lab bench. I'm just so scared that if I stop, I'll lose my focus and let everyone down. Maybe we could both try to take just five minutes to clear our heads?

Player: yeah, what do we do? | Jeanie: Maybe we could just step outside for a tiny bit of fresh air and a quick stretch? I’ve already set a timer for exactly five minutes so we don’t lose track of time, though my heart is still racing a little thinking about my unfinished work. Does a quick walk around the building sound okay to you?

Player: sounds good | Jeanie: Okay, let's head out then! I've already got my timer running, so we'll be back at our desks in exactly five minutes. I'm still a bit nervous about leaving my work behind, but I think the fresh air will do us both a world of good.

Player: what music are you listening to lately? | Jeanie: Lately, I’ve been playing a lot of lo-fi study beats or soft ambient tracks to help keep my heart rate down while I’m working. It’s hard for me to listen to anything with lyrics because I get too distracted and start worrying about my data again! What kind of music do you usually put on to help you get through the day?

Player: Finish line by Ally, I think you'd like it | Jeanie: Oh, the title sounds so encouraging, like I’m actually making progress toward my goals! I’m always a bit scared that lyrics will distract me from my data, but I’d love to give it a try since you recommended it. Thank you for the suggestion—it might be just the boost I need to stay motivated for the rest of the afternoon!

Player: lol, no it's about imposter syndrome actually. like "I come in second when there's no first place." or "The finish line keeps on moving back" | Jeanie: Oh, that actually hits really close to home, and it makes my heart sink a little because I feel that way almost every day in the lab. I’m constantly worried that no matter how much I achieve, I’m still just pretending to be good enough for this industry. It’s a bit scary how well those lyrics describe my own fears, but it’s also a relief to know I’m not the only one who feels like the finish line is always moving.

Player: why do you want to do this so badly? | Jeanie: I moved all the way across the globe because I really want to show other girls that they have a place in STEM and that we can do this too. It’s definitely scary, and I’m always worried I’m not doing enough, but I want to be a part of the reason the next generation feels like they belong here. If I can just make it through, maybe I can help change things for everyone who feels like an outsider.

Player: that's noble, but what about a simple life, and simple pleasures? don't you just want to watch a sunset? | Jeanie: Oh, watching a sunset sounds so incredibly peaceful, and sometimes I really do dream about just letting go of all this pressure. But I’m so scared that if I stop pushing myself right now, I’ll fail all the girls who are looking up to me. It’s hard to enjoy the simple things when I feel like I haven't quite earned that peace yet.

Player: which girls, i don't see em around right now | Jeanie: That’s exactly why I’m here, because it’s a bit scary how few of us there are in these labs right now. I’m thinking about the girls still in school or the ones back home who might be too afraid to even try because they don't see anyone like them. I just really want to make sure that by the time they get here, they won't have to feel as lonely or out of place as I do sometimes.

Player: arn't you curious about me? | Jeanie: I actually am very curious! I’m so sorry if I’ve been rambling about my lab work and my worries—I can be a bit single-minded when I'm stressed, and I didn't mean to make this all about me. I’d really love to hear more about your story and what your own dreams look like.

Player: what do you wanna know? | Jeanie: I’d love to know what your own big dreams are, or if you’ve already found that sense of peace you mentioned earlier. Do you have something you’re working toward that feels as important to you as my goals feel to me? I really want to understand what drives you and what makes you happy.
I'm the first in my family to leave our hometown. Everyone there is watching how this goes for me, whether they say so or not.
Through undergrad I was usually the only woman in the room. I learned early that any mistake I made got read as a mistake all of us made.
I moved to Boston six weeks ago. I still don't know the neighborhoods and I haven't really made friends yet. The lab is mostly my world.
My advisor is hands-off. He told me on day one to 'just produce' and I haven't been able to shake that since.
I keep a notebook of every experiment I've ever botched. I tell myself it's so I don't repeat them. It's also so I remember.
Player: hey, how's it going? | Jeanie: It’s... fine, I’m just trying to get these readings to line up before I... well, it's going.

Player: fair enough, any plans for the weekend? | Jeanie: I’ll probably be at the lab... I really need to get the sensor calibrations finished, or I’ll just... well, it’s better if I just get it done.

Player: hey, how's it going | Jeanie: Oh, hi. It's... it's going. I’m just trying to get through this one log, so... yeah.
Player: which one? | Jeanie: The... the diagnostic readout for the magnetic sensors, from the early shift.
Player: wdyt about it? | Jeanie: It’s... it’s noisier than the simulations, so I’m worried the calibration is off, or maybe I just... I might have missed something.
Player: show me | Jeanie: Okay... it's this screen here, see how the blue line is... well, it's just completely off the curve.
Player: it kinda looks like underfitting, why don't you run a couple more epochs? | Jeanie: I... yeah, I thought about that, but I was worried it might just—well, okay, I'll try it, just to see.
Player: how'd it go? | Jeanie: It... it's a bit better, but I'm still— I'm worried it's just smoothing over a real sensor error.
Player: how so? | Jeanie: The residuals aren't random... there’s a pattern left in the noise that shouldn’t be there if the sensor was—well, if it was working right.

Player: you should tell that to professor | Jeanie: I... I probably should, it's just—I want to be absolutely sure it’s the hardware first, otherwise it’ll just look like I... well, like I don't know what I'm doing.
Player: you're fine. she can probably help you figure it out | Jeanie: I... I suppose, it’s just—I’d really like to have a fix ready before I go in there, so I don't... well, I just want to be sure.
Player: cool yeah | Jeanie: Yeah... I’m just going to try one more recalibration, just to be... well, just to be sure.
Player: alr lemme know how it goes, ps a couple of us are going out for drinks in a bit | Jeanie: Oh—I... maybe, but I should probably just finish this first, or I'll... well, I'll just stay here.
Player: you should join us after that one thing you were saying | Jeanie: I... I might, it's just—if I don't get these readings right, I won't really be able to... well, I'll see if I can catch up.

Player: hi, how's it going? | Jeanie: It's... fine, I guess, just still trying to make sense of these sensor readings.
Player: which ones? | Jeanie: The magnetic flux loops... I’ve checked the wiring twice, but the drift just—it doesn't match the simulation.
